title: 'Marriage Story' Destroyed Us. Plus: Noah Baumbach on His Divorce Opus.
format: Sean and Amanda, a married man and a child of divorce, talk through Noah Baumbach's 'Marriage Story' and the thin canon of divorce films it joins. Then Sean interviews Baumbach about writing it.

segments.heading: 'Marriage Story'
segments.note: a married man and a divorce kid work through it

films.title: Marriage Story
films.note: Baumbach's 11th film, which both hosts think may be his best. Adam Driver's Charlie is a New York theater director, Scarlett Johansson's Nicole the actress who was his muse; the movie is less about marriage or divorce than about getting divorced: the lawyers, the custody math, the sad-dad apartment. Sean flags the common criticism that it tilts into Charlie's movie; Baumbach's answer is that Nicole owns the front half and Charlie the back. Standout scenes: Johansson's single-take monologue to Laura Dern, the mid-film fight, and Driver singing Sondheim's 'Being Alive.' Randy Newman score; Robbie Ryan shot it.

segments.heading: The divorce canon and Baumbach's filmography
segments.note: the movies that get compared to it

films.title: The Squid and the Whale
films.note: Baumbach's first, caustic divorce movie, told from the kid's perspective; the recurring reference point. Amanda calls it early Philip Roth to Marriage Story's late Roth.

films.title: Kramer vs. Kramer
films.note: The poster Marriage Story is meant to evoke; a movie whose gender politics play very differently now.

films.title: Mrs. Doubtfire
films.note: On Sean's short list of divorce movies, most of them told through the kid.

films.title: Shoot the Moon
films.note: Alan Parker's; a great, little-seen divorce film Baumbach pointed Sean to.

films.title: E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial
films.note: Read here as a divorce movie about a kid in the aftermath, and Baumbach's own double-feature pick to pair with Marriage Story.

films.title: Network
films.note: The bar for people at the top of their lungs; Sean's comparison for the film's big showdown.
Amanda's joke that the fight is shot like Zack Snyder, immediately walked back.

films.title: Greenberg
films.note: The L.A. movie that sets up Marriage Story's L.A.-ness, and the last of Baumbach's purely angry protagonists; where Greta Gerwig enters his filmography.

films.title: Frances Ha
films.note: Both hosts' No. 1 Baumbach: the one where the stand-in finally gets happiness, and a great homesick-for-New-York movie.

films.title: Mistress America
films.note: Amanda's husband loves it; she finds it kooky, part of the Preston Sturges / Billy Wilder phase.

films.title: While We're Young
films.note: Grouped with Mistress America as the kooky stretch.

films.title: The Meyerowitz Stories
films.note: The sister movie about aging and illness; Sean thinks Marriage Story is more accomplished but might like this one more.

films.title: Kicking and Screaming
films.note: Baumbach's debut about a smart, aimless college grad; Amanda's longtime favorite. Grover next to Charlie: a guy who wrecked a good relationship and doesn't know why. Not the 2005 Will Ferrell soccer comedy.

films.title: Margot at the Wedding
films.note: The mean one, full of characters who hate each other: the kind of movie he doesn't make anymore.

films.title: Lady Bird
films.note: Also opens with 'Being Alive,' sung at an audition (a detail Sean caught on a rewatch).

films.title: Phantom Thread
films.note: Sean's example of coded autobiography, versus the on-the-nose auto-fiction of 2019.

films.title: Pain and Glory
films.note: Almodóvar's; cited as a film that plainly is about its maker.

films.title: Parasite
films.note: The other movie this year that stuck with them, but about the world, where Marriage Story is about you and your partner.

films.title: Hannah and Her Sisters
films.note: The nearest Woody Allen touchstone; Marriage Story reads as the best non-Woody film in that lineage, only bigger and more direct.

films.title: Two English Girls
films.note: Truffaut; the use of music (the same cue playing joyful, then sad) was on Baumbach's mind for how Randy Newman scored this.

films.title: The Green Room
films.note: The last thing Baumbach saw, a Truffaut print at Metrograph, from a Henry James story about a man who can't get over the dead; he loved its panning shots.
